item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: FYI, there is no April 31st, not even in the Russian calendar used in 1904.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Got two potential links for this article. The first, since GRU-P lack their own GoI hub at the time of writing, would be to crosslink the mention of GRU-P to The Coldest War, which is arguably one of the best sources of GRU-P articles and tales on the site. Additionally, the tale The First Fifty-Five features Task Force ढ-3 (The Ides), and could serve as a crosslink as well. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

Should the Russian Space Agency or the GRU-P become aware of the true nature of SCP-2813, Orbital Task Force ढ-3 (The Ides) is to be deployed to secure the object in Foundation custody. ढ-3 is to maintain communication with SCP-2813-1, as well as provide for any maintenance of the object SCP-2813 instances themselves cannot provide. To ensure continued cooperation from SCP-2813 instances, limited amenities may be provided, when feasible, upon request.

To this:

Should the Russian Space Agency or the GRU-P become aware of the true nature of SCP-2813, Orbital Task Force ढ-3 (The Ides) is to be deployed to secure the object in Foundation custody. ढ-3 is to maintain communication with SCP-2813-1, as well as provide for any maintenance of the object SCP-2813 instances themselves cannot provide. To ensure continued cooperation from SCP-2813 instances, limited amenities may be provided, when feasible, upon request.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Awersome!

+1 myself and suggest so to a friends!

EDIT: have a Q to the author. Is Big Ship in Incident Log interstellar?
It's really headcanons that GRU P have a century-dead-scientist interstellar capacity

item.title: Re: Awesome! :D
item.content:encoded: I'm pretty sure that's what the author meant, rather than type out SCP-2813-1X for every instance, the comma there implies that B and C are also written in the same format, -1B or -1C.

item.title: Awesome! :D
item.content:encoded:
Excellent work! This item is very eerie and mysterious, although, while this is an insignificant error, I would advise changing this VERY small bit-
'SCP-2813 contains 3 individual non-corporeal operators (referred to as instances SCP-2813-1A, B, and C.).'
… to…
'SCP-2813 contains 3 individual non-corporeal operators (referred to as instances SCP-2813-1A, 1B and 1C.).'

Sorry. That's just the nitpicky side of me coming out XD

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For some reason this really reminds me of UC Gundam, though I always sort of thought that Zeon and Newtypism was inspired Konstantin's "But one cannot forever live in a cradle" quote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really like this. It was satisfying in a way a lot of other extraterrestrial skips fall just short of (for me). Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, despite a few very slight misgivings, (GRU's ancestor, the then Fourth Department (who itself descended from RU), the Abnormal Occurences Commission arose in 1935 independently from this - but that merely means that if at all, this particular segment of former RU and its findings/research might have been incorporated in later on, or simply lost in the sea of bureaucracy. Who knows, maybe it was that key personnel behind this project were eventually executed, for one. Overall, it introduces more possibilities and so on second thought I can't quite accuse it of contradicting canon), this is excellent, especially the diary, given that Konstantin Ciolkovskij was the pioneer of Russian and in fact world cosmology and together with Sergej Pavlovic Korolov make some of the most prominent heroes of my youth.

In short, this is good and you should feel good
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ahh, this is really great, just a thoroughly creative and original piece with a lot of depth.
Yussssss

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think part of Rush's misunderstanding is the term "Solar System Body":
That doesn't mean a separate solar system, it means "A chunk of something big enough to be worthy of note orbiting a star" such as a planet, comet, or a large asteroid. In the astronomical sense a centaur is the official term for anything that doesn't really fit the definition of a planet, stable minor planet, comet, or asteroid (ergo, its a hybrid).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love the Foundation-Russian anomalous space race. That's a really nice touch.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"somehow confuses with a distant solar system"

I'm not so sure about this. 13 Catherine is a somewhat standard designation for a small solar system body (most of the modern designations are like… 1995 HRU, or something like 4 Vesta).

As for the journal, Pincier wrote that but I believe it's supposed to be part of a word, rather than a misspelling of "to".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the inclusion of Russian historical figures, very nice

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"But afterwards [Illegible] ot an extension," from the Jan, 9 portion of the diary. Is that meant to be 'to'. Wasn't sure is the spelling was intentional.

It is thick and requires the addendum to be understood. Without the interviews and addendum this would be three men a on a rock in space that science somehow confuses with a distant solar system. I up-voted for the premise. I think the execution is at worst chaotic. At best, confusing. The article is promising.

item.title: Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Doctor Machesney, there's nothing wrong with posting your opinion on an SCP. If you want to leave critique, though, it does need to be at least a little bit specific and helpful. As Decibelle has already pointed out, what you've posted here is both unhelpful and actually flat-out inaccurate (censorship is not needed). Please have another look at our Criticism Policy.

item.title: Re: loved it!
item.content:encoded: This isn't good criticism and feedback. Not only are you just saying "I would censor more", but you don't give any examples of where it'd be valid. And generally, just randomly censoring is a bad thing to do, since when it's done wrong, it can be seen as lazy and frustrating to read.

item.title: loved it!
item.content:encoded:
i enjoyed reading this in depth scp. if i had one critiscism thohgh, i would censor more. other than that, one of the best i have ever read

*insert 10/10 joke here*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good work, gang. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Super special thanks to my co-author on this, Von Pincier. This is a part of our joint efforts towards a common concept we're working on. There's space involved, and stuff. Also, read this: http://www.scp-wiki.net/first55 . You'll like it, I promise.

Image is from NASA, modified a little to fit. http://ssd.jpl.nasa.gov/sbdb.cgi?sstr=2010%20XZ78;old=0;orb=1;cov=0;log=0;cad=0#orb
